Compare package versions on Bioconductor and GitHub

Author

Charlotte Soneson

Published

June 11, 2026

The goal of this analysis is to compare the package version of Bioconductor packages between the Bioconductor git server and the user repository. At the moment, for simplicity we limit ourselves to packages that have a GitHub repository indicated in the URL field in their DESCRIPTION file (a bit less than half of the packages in the current Bioconductor devel branch).

Load packages

suppressPackageStartupMessages({
    library(BiocPkgTools)
    library(DT)
    library(dplyr)
})

Retrieve data

# path to output file
outfile <- "pl_with_github_url.rds"

# set to TRUE to force retrieval of data even if outfile exists
force_retrieval <- FALSE

# helper functions
# ... get the GitHub repository from the URL field (which can contain multiple links)
.get_github_repo <- function(url) {
    # split string by spaces, commas, newlines
    url <- sub("/issues", "", trimws(unlist(strsplit(url, ", |\n|,\n|,"))))
    # grab only the entry pointing to a GitHub repository
    url <- url[grepl("github.com", url)]
    # extract repository name (if there are multiple GitHub repositories, 
    # choose the first one)
    sub("/$", "", sub("^.*github.com/(.+)$", "\\1", url)[1])
}

# ... attempt to read a DESCRIPTION file from the devel, main or master 
#     branch of a repo, and extract the version. If no DESCRIPTION file is 
#     found in these branches, return an empty string
.get_github_version <- function(repo) {
    desc <- tryCatch({
        read.dcf(url(paste0("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/", repo,
                            "/refs/heads/devel/DESCRIPTION")))
    }, error = function(e) {
        NULL
    })
    if (is.null(desc)) {
        desc <- tryCatch({
            read.dcf(url(paste0("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/", repo,
                                "/refs/heads/main/DESCRIPTION")))
        }, error = function(e) {
            NULL
        })
    }
    if (is.null(desc)) {
        desc <- tryCatch({
            read.dcf(url(paste0("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/", repo,
                                "/refs/heads/master/DESCRIPTION")))
        }, error = function(e) {
            NULL
        })
    }
    if (is.null(desc)) {
        version <- ""
    } else {
        version <- desc[1, "Version"]
    }
    version
}

# retrieve information about all Bioc packages, and check how many have a 
# GitHub repository in the URL field (we only analyze these further)
pl <- BiocPkgTools::biocPkgList()
table(url = grepl("github.com", pl$URL), 
      bugreports = grepl("github.com", pl$BugReports))
       bugreports
url     FALSE TRUE
  FALSE   993  325
  TRUE    137  932
if (!file.exists(outfile) || force_retrieval) {
    # retrieve version for packages with GitHub URL
    pl_with_github_url <- pl |> 
        dplyr::filter(grepl("github.com", URL) | 
                          grepl("github.com", BugReports)) |>
        dplyr::mutate(Repo1 = vapply(URL, .get_github_repo, ""),
                      Repo2 = vapply(BugReports, .get_github_repo, "")) |>
        dplyr::mutate(Repo = ifelse(Repo1 == "" | is.na(Repo1), Repo2, Repo1)) |>
        dplyr::mutate(GitHubVersion = vapply(Repo, .get_github_version, "")) |>
        dplyr::select(Package, Repo, Version, GitHubVersion)
    
    saveRDS(pl_with_github_url, outfile)
} else {
    pl_with_github_url <- readRDS(outfile)
}

# check for NA values or wrongly processed repositories
# repositories where the GitHub version is not found could be e.g. those where
# the package lives in a subfolder of the repository
table(is.na(pl_with_github_url$Repo))

FALSE 
 1394 
unname(grepv("github", pl_with_github_url$Repo))
[1] "igosungithub/HiCPotts"
table(pl_with_github_url$GitHubVersion == "")

FALSE  TRUE 
 1355    39 
DT::datatable(pl_with_github_url, 
              filter = list(position = "top", clear = FALSE),
              options = list(scrollX = TRUE, pageLength = 20))
# only keep packages where the GitHub version could be retrieved
pl_with_github_url <- pl_with_github_url |>
    dplyr::filter(GitHubVersion != "")
dim(pl_with_github_url)
[1] 1355    4
# convert the version numbers to numeric versions
pl_with_github_url$GitHubVersion <- 
    package_version(pl_with_github_url$GitHubVersion)
pl_with_github_url$Version <- 
    package_version(pl_with_github_url$Version)

Compare versions

# packages with matching version
table(pl_with_github_url$Version == 
          pl_with_github_url$GitHubVersion)

FALSE  TRUE 
 1075   280 
# packages with newer version on Bioconductor
table(pl_with_github_url$Version >
          pl_with_github_url$GitHubVersion)

FALSE  TRUE 
  305  1050 
# packages with newer version on GitHub
table(pl_with_github_url$Version <
          pl_with_github_url$GitHubVersion)

FALSE  TRUE 
 1330    25 
DT::datatable(pl_with_github_url |>
                  dplyr::filter(Version < GitHubVersion) |>
                  dplyr::mutate(Version = as.character(Version),
                                GitHubVersion = as.character(GitHubVersion)) |>
                  dplyr::select(-Repo),
              filter = list(position = "top", clear = FALSE),
              options = list(scrollX = TRUE, pageLength = 20))

Focus on packages with newer version on Bioconductor

pl_newer_on_bioc <- pl_with_github_url |> 
    dplyr::filter(Version > GitHubVersion) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
        major_diff = as.numeric(Version[, 1]) - as.numeric(GitHubVersion[, 1]), 
        minor_diff = as.numeric(Version[, 2]) - as.numeric(GitHubVersion[, 2]),
        patch_diff = as.numeric(Version[, 3]) - as.numeric(GitHubVersion[, 3]),
        github_major_version_0 = GitHubVersion[, 1] == "0"
    )
dim(pl_newer_on_bioc)
[1] 1050    8
DT::datatable(pl_newer_on_bioc |>
                  dplyr::mutate(Version = as.character(Version),
                                GitHubVersion = as.character(GitHubVersion)) |>
                  dplyr::select(-Repo),
              filter = list(position = "top", clear = FALSE),
              options = list(scrollX = TRUE, pageLength = 20))
# check whether packages with newer version on Bioconductor have patch 
# version 0 (this could, but doesn't have to, indicate that no changes have 
# been made to the package code upstream - at least not in the current branch)
table(as.numeric(as.character(pl_newer_on_bioc$Version[, 3])))

   0    1    2    3    4    5   23   32 
1020   21    3    2    1    1    1    1 
# check the difference in the major/minor version
# stratify based on whether the package version on GitHub is corresponding 
# to a "new submission" (major version = 0) or not
table(major_diff = pl_newer_on_bioc$major_diff, 
      minor_diff_sign = sign(pl_newer_on_bioc$minor_diff), 
      github_major_version_0 = pl_newer_on_bioc$github_major_version_0)
, , github_major_version_0 = FALSE

          minor_diff_sign
major_diff  -1   0   1
         0   0   1 863
         1   8   1   2
         2   0   0   0

, , github_major_version_0 = TRUE

          minor_diff_sign
major_diff  -1   0   1
         0   0   2   1
         1 169   0   2
         2   0   0   1
# some unexpected cases
pl_newer_on_bioc |>
    dplyr::filter(github_major_version_0 & 
                      sign(minor_diff) != -1) |>
    dplyr::select(Package, Version, GitHubVersion) |>
    dplyr::mutate(Version = as.character(Version),
                  GitHubVersion = as.character(GitHubVersion))
# A tibble: 6 × 3
  Package                    Version GitHubVersion
  <chr>                      <chr>   <chr>        
1 cellNexus                  0.99.23 0.99.8       
2 ChromSCape                 1.23.0  0.0.0.9000   
3 GGPA                       1.25.0  0.9.6        
4 illuminaio                 0.55.0  0.43.0       
5 panoramic                  0.99.3  0.99.1       
6 SingleMoleculeFootprinting 2.7.0   0.0.1

Conclusions

  • Many package maintainers appear not to sync their GitHub repositories to pull down changes made upstream (e.g. version bumps or other edits made by the core team). However, it is important to note that this doesn’t mean that the package content is different - a more thorough analysis would be needed to check if this is actually the case. Among the packages with a newer version available on Bioconductor, the majority have patch version equal to 0 on Bioconductor, indicating that no changes were made to the package in the current (devel) branch. Of course, this does not mean that no changes were made upstream since the last synchronization with the local GitHub repository.
  • Most local changes are pushed upstream - only few packages have a higher version number on GitHub, and in most cases the difference is small (likely indicating ongoing development).
